docs.title: Introduction
docs.text:
Kubernetes (K8s)

Before beginning these notes, ensure you have completed my notes and exercises on Docker.

In my exercises on Docker, we saw how to create and deploy containers; orchestration. Docker-Compose is appropriate for doing development on a single host.

In a production environment, this may need to be done at scale. Kubernetes (K8s) allows us to deploy, scale and manage across multiple hosts:

K8s lets us

  • Deploy containers in an automated manner
  • Monitor their state
  • Take management actions if they crash or go down or need replacement
  • Scale up containers, create more instances to cope with load, and the converse
  • Load balancing across multiple instances
  • Manage networking and storage

Docker-Compose allowed us to manage multi-container projects. K8s could be considered as an orchestration system across multiple machines.

Services like AWS ECS may be useful in some of these areas, but it takes some control away from us. Vendor Lock-in! K8s is open source and gives us orchestration independent of platform. It allows us to script to any cloud provider or to our own servers in private cloud. Rewriting this in 2026, sovereignty has become a critical concern.

A K8s configuration file is a standardized example of infrastructure as code.

As always, consider my notes a summary and a starting point. Definitive information may be found at the Kubernetes web site.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text: K8s is a combination of tools, concepts and architecture. You must manually set up the cluster. Ideally, you already have skills with VMs, Networking, etc.

docs.title: Pod
docs.text: In K8s, a pod is the smallest entity we consider, it may just hold a single container, or multiple containers.

docs.title: Worker Node
docs.text: A pod runs on a worker node. I might stand up a Ubuntu server to act as a worker node. I may run many pods on a worker node. As a starting point, you need to create at least one worker node. You may need multiple worker nodes for an application. Worker nodes are managed by the Master Node using a kubelet, the communications system. A kube-proxy manages communications.

docs.title: Proxy
docs.text:
K8s sets up a proxy on each worker node to control networking from outside the host to the pods.

Fig 1. Worker Node.

docs.title: Master Node
docs.text: You do not control anything via worker nodes. The control plane of K8s works via a master node. On a simple system with one worker node, you may colocate the master node.

docs.title: Cluster
docs.text:
An assemblage with a master node and worker node(s) is a K8s cluster.

Fig 2. K8s Cluster.
